event: Christ mocked and tormented in silence
symbols: seated Christ in silence — the patient endurance of the Lamb who is led to slaughter
crown of thorns — the mockery of kingship that paradoxically reveals true sovereignty
halo amidst mockery — divine glory persisting through human degradation
tormentors surrounding Christ — the forces of evil encircling the innocent one
whip on the ground — the instruments of torture and the violence already inflicted
gray/grisaille palette — the draining of color reflects the draining of life, the sobriety of suffering
description: A grisaille painting in muted gray tones showing Christ seated centrally, nearly nude, wearing a crown of thorns and a radiant halo. He appears bound, with a weary, resigned expression. Around him stand several figures in white and gray garments — soldiers and tormentors — some striking or mocking him. One figure to the left holds a staff or spear, while others crowd close. A whip or rope lies on the green floor in the foreground. The predominantly gray palette gives the scene a ghostly, haunting quality, with only Christ's halo providing any sense of light or glory.
theological_description: This scene from the Passion depicts the mocking and abuse of Christ during his trial, as recorded in Matthew 27:27-31 and Mark 15:16-20. The soldiers 'stripped him and put a scarlet robe on him, and twisting together a crown of thorns, they put it on his head... and they knelt before him and mocked him, saying, Hail, King of the Jews!' The grisaille rendering — drained of color — powerfully conveys the theological reality of kenosis, the self-emptying of Philippians 2:6-8: Christ 'emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in human likeness.' His silence before his tormentors fulfills Isaiah 53:7: 'He was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter.' The paradox is profound: the one being mocked as king is in truth the King of Kings. The crown of thorns, placed in derision, becomes the true crown — for Christ's kingship is revealed precisely through suffering. First Peter 2:23 reflects on this scene: 'When he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten, but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly.'

scripture_refs.reason: The soldiers mocking Christ with the crown of thorns and scarlet robe — the event depicted
Like a lamb led to slaughter, he opened not his mouth — Christ's silence before his tormentors
Christ emptied himself, taking the form of a servant — the kenosis made visible in his humiliation
When reviled he did not revile in return — the patient endurance depicted in Christ's silence
Mark's account of the soldiers mocking and striking Jesus
